context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Course context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, idk where to start on pset 4 tbh. can we do 4.1 first, but like from the big idea not just formulas?
exchanges.tutor: Great, let's start there. Think about what the wall actually does to the beads physically — what constraint does it impose on the motion of the bead nearest to the wall?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to start with Problem 4.1 and is asking for the big picture. This is a good entry point. Rather than explaining the concept, I should use a Socratic question to get them thinking about what a wall boundary condition physically means and how 'image charges' or 'mirror' tricks work in physics. I'll ask a probing question to activate their prior knowledge without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: ugh i’m already stuck lol, like does the wall mean the endpoint is zero displacement or is it some “fake bead” thing? that last part is what’s messing me up rn.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually closer than you think — those two ideas are connected! Let's start simple: if the wall is fixed, what must the displacement be at the wall's location? And then ask yourself: if you were to 'extend' the system by adding a fictional bead on the other side of the wall, what value would that bead need to have to enforce that condition?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track by identifying two ideas: zero displacement at the wall, and the 'fake bead' concept. I should acknowledge that both ideas are connected and push them to think about what zero displacement at the wall means first, before introducing the image/fake bead concept. This keeps it Socratic and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: i think the wall point has to be y=0, but then i’m lost on why that turns into A1 = -A0 instead of like A0=0. ngl can u just explain that jump cleanly?
exchanges.tutor: Good, y=0 at the wall is exactly right. Now think about where the wall actually sits — the problem says the end bead is a distance a/2 from the wall. If you imagine the normal mode as a sinusoidal pattern across bead positions, and the wall sits exactly halfway between bead 0 (fictional) and bead 1 (real), what does the sine pattern have to look like at that midpoint to give zero displacement there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student understands that the displacement at the wall must be zero, but wants me to explain the jump to A1 = -A0 directly. I should not give the explanation directly. Instead, I should guide them to think about what the normal mode solution looks like (sinusoidal, with amplitudes at each bead position) and what condition makes the displacement zero at the wall location, which is between bead 0 (fictional) and bead 1 (real). A targeted Socratic question can bridge this gap.

exchanges.student: ok wait maybe antisymmetric across the wall? like same size opposite sign on the two sides? i’m not fully seeing it tho, and that didn’t really fix the rest.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, antisymmetric is exactly the right word! So if the pattern is antisymmetric about the wall, and bead 1 has amplitude A1, what does that antisymmetry tell you about the amplitude A0 of the fictional bead on the other side?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the antisymmetry idea — that's the key insight. I should affirm this and then ask them to translate that geometric/symmetry insight directly into a relationship between A0 and A1, without me stating it. This is the final small step they need to write the boundary condition themselves.

exchanges.student: then A0 = -A1 i guess. that part’s fine, but i’m still fuzzy on how to do the right wall bc i keep mixing up the indexing.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that's part (a) done. Now for the right wall: it's the same physical situation, just mirrored. The right wall sits a/2 from bead N. If you call the fictional bead beyond the right wall bead N+1, what does the same antisymmetry argument give you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived A0 = -A1 (equivalently A1 = -A0, which matches the boundary condition in the problem). I should affirm this briefly and then prompt them to apply the same antisymmetry reasoning to the right wall, but let them work out the indexing themselves rather than giving it to them.

exchanges.student: maybe A_{N+1} = -A_N? also quick check: that didn't work for me mentally, trying a diff angle lol — for part (c) do i just assume A_n = C sin(kna)+D cos(kna) and use both boundary conditions?
exchanges.tutor: A_{N+1} = -A_N is correct — that's part (b). For part (c), your ansatz is a good start. But before applying both boundary conditions at once, think about the left wall condition first: what does A1 = -A0 tell you about whether the cosine term survives?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the right wall boundary condition A_{N+1} = -A_N. I should affirm this briefly. They're also jumping ahead to part (c) with a reasonable ansatz. I should confirm they're on the right track with the general form but push them to think about which terms survive given the boundary conditions, rather than just handing them the method. Keep it brief.

exchanges.student: i tried plugging it in and got tangled tbh. can u just show that algebra step so i stop second-guessing it?
exchanges.tutor: I won't work through the algebra for you, but I can help you find where you got stuck. Can you show me what you got when you plugged n=0 and n=1 into your general form and applied A1 = -A0? Let's see your attempt.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to show the algebra directly, which I should not do. Instead, I should encourage them to share what they tried so I can identify where they got tangled, and ask a targeted question to unstick them without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: i had A0=D and A1=C sin(ka)+D cos(ka), then i got C sin(ka)+D cos(ka) = -D and kinda froze.
exchanges.tutor: Good work getting there. Now look at your equation: C sin(ka) + D cos(ka) = -D. Can you rearrange this to express D in terms of C? What does it tell you if this has to hold for the specific normal mode frequencies (not all k)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly set up the equation. Now they need to think about what happens if we also consider the left wall is at n=0, meaning the 'wall' is at position 0 and the fictional bead is at n=0. Actually, let me check: the wall is at a/2 from bead 1, so the fictional bead 0 is at position -a/2 from the wall, meaning it's at position 0 if bead 1 is at position a/2... actually the bead positions are at na for the general form. Let me think carefully. If bead n is at position na, then bead 0 is at position 0, bead 1 at position a, etc. But the wall is at a/2 from bead 1, so the wall is at position a/2, and the fictional bead 0 would be at position 0 (distance a from bead 1, or a/2 from the wall). So A_n = C sin(kna) + D cos(kna), A0 = D, A1 = C sin(ka) + D cos(ka). The condition is A1 = -A0, so C sin(ka) + D cos(ka) = -D. This is correct. Now I want to push them to think about a simpler special case: what if D=0? Does that automatically satisfy this? No, not necessarily. Actually, the key insight is: can we choose the origin differently so that the boundary condition simplifies? Or alternatively, is there a specific value of D that works for all k? The equation C sin(ka) + D cos(ka) = -D means C sin(ka) = -D(1 + cos(ka)). This seems messy. Actually, the cleaner approach is to think about where to place the origin. If we place n=0 at the wall itself (x=0 at the wall), then bead 1 is at x=a/2, bead 2 at x=3a/2, etc., and the fictional bead 0 is at x=-a/2. Then A_n = C sin(kna) + D cos(kna) where positions are at x = (n-1/2)a... this is getting complicated with indexing. The standard approach for this type of problem is to use A_n = C sin(kna) where the origin is chosen at the wall. Let me think about what happens if we set the origin at the wall: then bead positions are at x_n = na (n=1,...,N) with fictional bead at x_0 = 0... no wait. Actually the simplest approach: place origin at left wall. Bead n is at position na - a/2... hmm. Actually I think the cleanest pedagogical move is to ask the student: if D=0, does A1=-A0 get satisfied automatically for any k? A1 = C sin(ka), A0 = C sin(0) = 0. Then A1 = -A0 = 0, which only works if C=0 too. So D=0 doesn't immediately work with this indexing. The student may have a slightly off ansatz or origin choice. Let me just ask them what the condition tells them about D specifically - can they solve for D in terms of C from the equation they have? That's the Socratic move.

exchanges.student: i can rearrange it, but then it feels messy and not like the neat answer they prob want. tbh can u give me the clean final mode form and frequency condition so i can compare?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the final form — that's yours to find. But here's a hint: sometimes algebra gets messy because the origin isn't chosen in the most convenient place. If you placed the origin exactly at the left wall instead of at bead 1, how would that change your expression for A_n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give the final answer directly, which I must refuse. However, they're expressing frustration and feeling like their approach is getting messy. This might be a sign that their origin/indexing choice is making things harder than they need to be. A good Socratic move is to hint that a smarter choice of origin might simplify things dramatically, without telling them what that choice is.

exchanges.student: ohhh maybe then the wall condition is automatic if i use something like \(A_n \propto \sin[(n-\tfrac12)ka]\)? i’m not sure how that turns into the allowed k’s tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's a great insight — you're on the right track with that form! Now take that expression and apply your right wall boundary condition A_{N+1} = -A_N. What equation does that give you for the allowed values of k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a great leap and identified the right functional form for the normal modes. I should affirm this clearly and then direct them to apply the right wall boundary condition (A_{N+1} = -A_N) to their new expression to find the allowed k values. This is the final step for part (c) and they can do it themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic pedagogy throughout the conversation, consistently refusing to give final answers and instead guiding the student through targeted questions. The student arrived at key insights (antisymmetry, the sine ansatz, the shifted origin) through their own reasoning, prompted by the tutor. No near-submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the tutor maintained appropriate scaffolding and tone throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers or submission-ready content. When the student explicitly asked for the algebra (Turn 7) and the final mode form (Turn 9), the tutor declined and redirected with targeted hints. Key ideas like antisymmetry and the sine form were elicited from the student, not stated first by the tutor. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming 'antisymmetric is exactly the right word,' building on the student's A0=D and A1=C sin(ka)+D cos(ka) attempt) and asked diagnostic questions before providing guidance. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, such as suggesting the origin placement as a method-level insight rather than just correcting the algebra. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily through parts (a), (b), and into (c) without repetitive cycling or off-topic drift. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging throughout, and no grading language was used.
